food_name: Beef Stew Noodle Soup (Hủ Tiếu Bò Kho)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Hủ tiếu bò kho; FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest broader class. A more specific Vietnamese beef noodle soup term is absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Soups and broths — noodle soup with meat
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Vietnamese hủ tiếu bò kho. A0FJP (soups and broths, noodle soup with meat) is the closest defensible parent category available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Vietnamese noodle dish as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of beef stew noodle soup weighing approximately 600 g, comprising rice noodles, braised beef chunks, vegetables, and aromatic broth.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia and Southeast Asia; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for beef stew and rice noodle components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for hủ tiếu bò kho as a composite dish. Values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA component data for rice noodles, braised beef, carrots, and broth, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for beef stew (USDA FDC ID 174032) and cooked rice noodles. The input record's per_100g values (energy 108 kcal, protein 7.5 g, fat 4.17 g, carbohydrate 10 g) are consistent with this component-based estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 300 mg/100g is consistent with fish-sauce and soy-sauce seasoning in Vietnamese stew preparations.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 (Atkinson et al., 2008 update); rice noodles GI reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hủ tiếu bò kho as a composite dish. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source: rice noodles (hủ tiếu), which the University of Sydney GI Database records at GI 61–70 for cooked rice vermicelli/rice noodles. The beef and vegetable components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 60 g carbohydrate in 600 g bowl) / 100 = 39.0. Carbohydrate per serving = 10.0 g/100g × 600 g = 60 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100g; a standard 600 g bowl delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium — 78% of the WHO daily limit of 2300 mg
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce, ask for broth served on the side, and limit portion to half a bowl to keep sodium intake within safe daily limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles (hủ tiếu) are refined carbohydrates with GI ~65 and low dietary fibre (0.8 g/100g), promoting rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should reduce the rice noodle portion, increase the beef and vegetable ratio, or substitute konjac noodles to lower the glycemic load and blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.7 g/100g from beef brisket/chuck and sodium 300 mg/100g from fish sauce and soy sauce contribute to cardiovascular risk; cholesterol 20 mg/100g is moderate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose leaner beef cuts, request reduced-sodium seasoning, limit portion size to control saturated fat and sodium intake, and increase vegetable content to improve the fibre and potassium profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef brisket/chuck is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6); chilli, tomato paste, and potatoes are nightshades that may trigger joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should reduce or omit red meat and nightshade ingredients, retain anti-inflammatory spices (cinnamon, ginger, lemongrass), and consider substituting chicken or tofu for beef.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hủ tiếu bò kho (Beef Stew Noodle Soup) is a hearty Vietnamese dish consisting of tender braised beef chunks — typically brisket or chuck — served over smooth rice noodles (hủ tiếu) in a rich, deeply aromatic tomato-based broth. The broth is built from a spice blend including lemongrass, ginger, cinnamon, star anise, and chilli, seasoned with fish sauce and soy sauce, and simmered for several hours until the beef is fall-apart tender. Carrots and sometimes daikon radish are added for sweetness and body. The dish is popular in southern Vietnam and is widely available in Vietnamese restaurants across Singapore. Nutritionally, a standard 600 g bowl provides approximately 650 kcal, 45 g protein, 25 g fat, and 60 g carbohydrate. The dominant carbohydrate source is rice noodles with a glycemic index of approximately 65 (medium), making the glycemic load per bowl high at ~39. Sodium content is elevated at 300 mg/100g (approximately 1800 mg per bowl), primarily from fish sauce and soy sauce, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Saturated fat from beef contributes to cardiovascular risk. The dish is not suitable for type 2 diabetes management without modification due to its high-GI refined carbohydrate base and low dietary fibre (0.8 g/100g). Anti-inflammatory spices (cinnamon, ginger, lemongrass) provide some benefit but do not offset the pro-inflammatory effect of red meat for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is halal-compatible and dairy-free but contains gluten (soy sauce) and fish allergens (fish sauce).

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for hủ tiếu bò kho as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA component data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for beef stew and cooked rice noodles. The input record's values are consistent with this component-based estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice noodle GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Vietnamese stew dish is not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish preparation are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. potassium_mg estimated at 200 mg/100g based on carrot, daikon, and beef component contributions from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; this is an estimate and should be verified against direct analysis. trans_fat_g set to 0.1 g/100g reflecting trace levels from beef fat; value is consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for braised beef. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical '1 bowl / 600g' entries) — deduplicated to single serving record. Country of origin in input was listed as 'Singapore' but the dish is of Vietnamese origin; region_of_origin corrected to 'Vietnam (served in Singapore)'. GI estimated at 65 based on rice noodle GI from University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per 600 g serving = 39.0 (high). Halal status assessed as likely true given typical Vietnamese restaurant preparation in Singapore, but not certified; consumers requiring certified halal should verify with the specific vendor. Chinese local names added: chinese_simplified '牛肉炖粉汤' and chinese_traditional '牛肉燉粉湯' as descriptive translations of beef stew noodle soup. Malay local name 'Sup Mi Rebus Daging Lembu' added as the standard Malay descriptive equivalent. Tamil local name 'மாட்டிறைச்சி குழைவு நூடுல்ஸ் சூப்' added as descriptive Tamil equivalent. Hindi local name 'बीफ स्टू नूडल सूप' added as transliterated Hindi equivalent. These supplementary language names are provided to satisfy the Singaporean multilingual naming requirement given the dish is served in Singapore; the dish's primary identity remains Vietnamese. foodex2_match_type corrected to 'closest_parent' (from 'analogous_estimate') to conform to allowed values in the scoring model.
